channel.title: Drug-Discovery on Athan Dial - Portfolio
channel.description: Recent content in Drug-Discovery on Athan Dial - Portfolio

item.title: Data Quality Is an Incident Discipline
item.description:
Context

A single misspelled configuration field quietly pushed six FDA-approved compounds through three discovery programs and wasted about $90, but the real damage was the doubt it sowed in a pipeline that controls six-figure purchasing decisions. The production data platform powering dashboards, nomination tools, and leadership reviews ingested external sources, applied layered transformations, and surfaced boolean flags that dictated procurement. The underlying problem: data quality failures in applied AI workflows rarely explode loudly. They produce plausible numbers that lie quietly until a scientist notices a weird compound on a list months later. Without structured incident response, those errors erode trust faster than they burn dollars.

item.title: Training Data as Product: Building a Reusable Dataset Pipeline
item.description:
Context

Our cyclic peptide program had the modeling stack in place, but lacked the training data to run it. The modeling team was standing by, but no applied AI training dataset existed for this therapeutic protein target. Public bioactivity coverage was sparse, and the obvious starting point—ChEMBL—didn’t have sufficient relevant data.

The richest signal source was a published patent: dozens of pages of compound tables, activity annotations, and structural descriptions. Extracting that into usable training data meant making judgment calls that affect downstream behavior: what counts as a positive, what as a negative, how to represent structure, how to manage class imbalance, and how to preserve provenance so results survive scientific review during nomination.

item.title: Operating Discipline in Compound Nominations
item.description:
Context

Each nomination wave locked $30K–$100K of procurement and months of wet-lab capacity. A sloppy compound picklist translated directly into sunk experimental runs with no way to learn from the outcome.

The company’s computational drug discovery group could produce ranked compound lists on demand, yet every screening cycle restarted from zero. Scientists ran their own filters, mixed vendor catalogs with local enumerations, and argued from memory because knowledge lived in a system or in someone’s head. Assay readouts landed six months later with no mechanism to attribute success or failure back to the initial decision. The consequence: we could not learn whether structural diversity constraints mattered, whether AI rankings were overfitting, or whether procurement dollars were allocated to the right exploration vs. exploitation balance.
